turns.content: I'm trying to deserialize a JSON array where the elements can be either strings or numbers, but I get this error: index out of bounds. It seems like serde is treating everything as one type instead of allowing mixed types in the array.

That error happens because you're likely trying to use a tagged enum or a fixed-size vector when your JSON actually contains heterogeneous data. For this specific case, you need an untagged enum with variants for String and f64, which lets serde_json::Value handle the polymorphism.

I tried defining a struct with two fields and let it derive Deserialize, but that only works if every element has both values. What's the right way to define an untagged enum for this so serde_json::Value can be used as a fallback?

You should use an untagged enum where each variant matches one possible JSON type, like Result<String, f64>, and then deserialize into a Vec<serde_json::Value> if the array doesn't strictly conform to those variants.
Wait, I thought untagged enums require all variants to be present in every JSON object. Is that really how it works here or am I misunderstanding something?
No, that's a misconception. An untagged enum doesn't enforce presence of all variants; instead, serde infers the correct variant based on the JSON type (string vs number) without needing tags.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to deserialize a JSON array where every element might be either an integer or a string, but my usual struct with Option<T> keeps failing. When I tried using a Vec<String>, it only accepted strings, and when I used a Vec<i32>, it only accepted integers. It feels like serde is just too rigid for this kind of polymorphic data.

That's a very common frustration when moving from typed languages to Rust with Serde. The issue is that standard enums require explicit tag names or variants, which you won't have if the JSON array just mixes types without labels. For heterogeneous arrays like this, you need an untagged enum where each variant matches a specific type in your schema. You can define something like MyEnum with i32 and String variants, then use serde_json::Value as a fallback when neither matches perfectly.

Okay, so I should just create an untagged enum? But what if the JSON has nested structures or arrays within those elements? Like, sometimes it's not just a raw number but an object with properties. How do I handle that without breaking my deserialization logic?

Exactly, and that's where the Vec<serde_json::Value> pattern becomes essential. Instead of trying to map every possible nested structure into a single enum variant, you deserialize each array element individually using serde_json::from_value(). This lets you handle raw numbers, strings, objects, or even nested arrays without needing to define exhaustive variants for everything. You can then inspect the resulting Value to decide how to process it downstream.

So I'm basically bypassing strict type checking by accepting serde_json::Value first, then branching based on what's inside? That sounds like a hack. Is there any downside to this approach compared to defining explicit types upfront?

It is more flexible than rigid enums, but yes, it does sacrifice compile-time safety for runtime flexibility. The trade-off is that you lose some type-level guarantees until you manually validate the Value after deserialization. However, since you're building an automation tool, this approach avoids writing repetitive if-else chains for every possible JSON shape and keeps your code DRY.

Got it. So instead of wrestling with Option<T> or tagged enums, I use serde_json::Value for each item in the array, then map them to my internal types later based on their actual content? That makes sense.

Precisely. You deserialize the entire array as Vec<serde_json::Value>, then iterate through and convert each entry to your specific domain type using pattern matching or manual inspection. This gives you maximum adaptability without needing to anticipate every future data shape your API might send.

Wow, that's the missing piece I was stuck on all morning. I had been trying to force-fit everything into a single enum and it just wouldn't work. Now I can parse any mix of types in one go without rewriting my logic for each new field type.

That's the beauty of using serde_json::Value as a universal placeholder. It lets your automation tool stay robust even when the input schema evolves, as long as you handle the Value inspection correctly in your downstream processing logic.
